skill.name: browser-use
skill.description: Automates browser interactions through the browser-use CLI for navigation, testing, screenshots, form entry, authenticated browsing, and web data extraction.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions from Claude, Codex, and Claude Code using the browser-use CLI.
skill.author: browser-use

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer overcounted markdown inline code and fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution, so those detections are mostly false positives. The skill still has high inherent risk because it authorizes browser automation over real Chrome profiles, cookie export and import, cloud profile sync, remote sessions, public session sharing, tunnels, JavaScript evaluation, and Python execution. No evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it is not safe to publish without strong warnings and review.

high_findings.title: Browser Credential and Cookie Exposure
high_findings.description: The skill documents use of real Chrome profiles, cookie inspection, cookie export and import, and full profile sync. This can expose login sessions, tracking cookies, and other browser credentials, especially when synced to a cloud profile.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly describe real Chrome profile use, cookie commands, profile sync, and full-profile sync containing every session token. This is legitimate functionality, but it is a clear high-impact data exposure risk.

high_findings.title: Remote Browser Sessions May Expose Authenticated Data
high_findings.description: The skill supports cloud-hosted browsers, remote tasks, cloud profiles, and public session sharing. Authenticated pages or sensitive task output could be visible outside the local machine if users select remote mode or share sessions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Remote mode, cloud profile use, live URLs, and session sharing are explicit in the documentation. The risk depends on user choices, so confidence is high but not critical.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Browser JavaScript and Python Execution
high_findings.description: The skill allows JavaScript evaluation in pages and Python execution through the browser-use CLI, including execution from a file. These capabilities can read page data, automate sensitive actions, or execute unsafe local logic if misused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly includes browser-use eval and browser-use python commands. The security impact is real, although the commands are part of the advertised automation use case.

medium_findings.title: Local Development Tunnel Exposure
medium_findings.description: The skill can expose a local development server through a Cloudflare tunnel and browse it from a cloud browser. This may unintentionally publish local services or internal test data if users tunnel the wrong port.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Tunnel commands and a local-server workflow are directly documented. The exposure is user-initiated and cleanup is documented, so this is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Temporary Cookie Files
medium_findings.description: The workflow shows exporting cookies to /tmp/cookies.json before importing them into a remote profile. Temporary cookie files can be read by other processes or left behind after the task.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The specific temporary cookie file path is present in the documented workflow. The risk is bounded because this is an example, not mandatory behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external command findings are markdown inline code or fenced command examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They document browser-use CLI syntax rather than hidden shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are visible markdown documentation and fenced bash examples. They do not contain executable Ruby code or concealed command substitution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are project documentation links and placeholder example domains used to explain navigation, tunnels, and authenticated browsing. No evidence found that the skill silently sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as documentation links or user-visible examples. I did not find evidence of hidden exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections appear to be substring matches inside ordinary words such as bash, flash, or hash-related documentation text. No evidence found of cryptographic code or weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown command documentation, not a cryptographic implementation. The static labels do not match the semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Browser-Use Bash Command Surface
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to browser-use commands. This is necessary for the skill, but it gives the assistant a broad command surface for browser state, cookies, profiles, tunnels, and remote sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command catalog are explicit. The pattern is dangerous by capability, not by hidden intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Full Profile Sync to Cloud
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes full profile sync and warns that it includes all cookies, tracking cookies, and every session token. This is a high-value credential movement pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly states that full sync includes every session token. This directly confirms the sensitive data movement concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
dangerous_patterns.description: I searched for override, ignore-instruction, preapproval, administrator, and role-change phrases. No evidence found of prompt injection text in the reviewed skill file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scan covered common prompt-injection indicators, and the file appears to be operational documentation. Confidence is not higher because absence of evidence is limited to the reviewed text.

content.value_statement: Manual web testing and data collection can be slow and hard to repeat. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured browser-use workflow for navigation, screenshots, forms, profiles, and extraction.
actual_capabilities: Opens pages, moves through browser history, scrolls pages, and switches tabs through the browser-use CLI.
Lists page state and clickable element indexes before clicking, typing, selecting, hovering, or sending keys.
Captures screenshots and retrieves page titles, text, HTML, values, attributes, and element bounding boxes.
Runs JavaScript snippets and browser-use Python commands for advanced inspection and automation.
Manages local, real Chrome, and remote browser modes, including profiles, tasks, sessions, and cloud profiles.
Creates tunnels so remote browsers can reach local development servers when explicitly requested.
limitations: Requires the browser-use CLI to be installed and configured before the skill can work.
Authenticated browsing can expose cookies or session data if profiles or cloud sync are used carelessly.
Element indexes can change after page navigation, scrolling, or dynamic content updates.
Remote tasks, cloud profiles, tunnels, and public session links depend on external services and network access.

use_cases.title: Test a Web Application Flow
use_cases.description: Navigate a local or deployed app, inspect page state, fill forms, take screenshots, and verify success messages.

use_cases.title: Collect Structured Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Open target pages, extract visible text or selected HTML, and capture screenshots for repeatable review.

use_cases.title: Operate an Authenticated Web Tool
use_cases.description: Use an approved browser profile to perform logged-in tasks while confirming profile choice and cookie handling first.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-use to open the target URL, run state, summarize the page title, visible actions, and the next safe step.

prompt_templates.title: Fill and Verify a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-use to inspect the form, fill the requested fields, submit it, and verify the result with state and a screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Page Information
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-use to open the page, extract the relevant text or selected HTML, and return the findings with source context.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Authenticated Remote Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced profile and cloud session use
prompt_templates.prompt: Ask which browser mode and profile to use, confirm any cookie sync, run the remote task, report status, and stop sessions when done.

output_examples.input: Open my staging app and check whether the signup button is visible.
output_examples.output: The page opened successfully. The signup button is visible in the primary navigation, and a screenshot was captured for confirmation.

output_examples.input: Use the page state to fill the contact form and confirm submission.
output_examples.output: The form fields were identified, completed, and submitted. The confirmation message appeared after submission.

output_examples.input: Check an authenticated dashboard using my selected profile.
output_examples.output: The selected profile was used after confirmation. The dashboard loaded, key status values were reviewed, and no profile sync was performed.

best_practices: Run browser-use state before interactions, because element indexes are required and can change.
Ask before using real Chrome profiles, cloud profiles, cookie sync, tunnels, or public session links.
Stop browser sessions, cloud sessions, and tunnels after the task is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not sync a full browser profile when one domain-specific cookie set is enough.
Do not export cookies to temporary files unless the user explicitly approves the workflow.
Do not run JavaScript, Python, or remote agent tasks on sensitive pages without clear user intent.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, page inspection, interactions, screenshots, data extraction, sessions, profiles, and selected remote browser workflows.

faq.question: Does it need browser-use installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The browser-use CLI must be installed and configured before the skill can run its commands.

faq.question: Can it use my existing logins?
faq.answer: Yes, if you approve use of a real Chrome profile or synced cloud profile. This can expose sensitive cookies.

faq.question: Can it run against local development servers?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use local browser mode directly, or create a tunnel for remote browser access when approved.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill can handle cookies, profiles, remote sessions, tunnels, JavaScript evaluation, and Python execution. These features need careful permission control.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious intent?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The risks come from powerful documented capabilities, not from hidden malicious behavior in the reviewed file.
